turns.content: I'm trying to set up a workspace with multiple crates, but I thought the root Cargo.toml would automatically pull in every dependency from all sub-crates. It feels like it should just be one big list at the top.

Actually, that's not how workspace inheritance works. The dependencies defined in your root Cargo.toml are only inherited by crates that explicitly declare them via [patch] or [workspace.dependencies]. If a sub-crate doesn't have those declarations, it won't see the overrides unless you add them individually.
That makes sense. So if I want to update all versions across my entire workspace at once using cargo update, will that work even with inheritance?

Yes, cargo update respects the workspace configuration and can propagate changes through inherited dependencies. But you need to be careful because if a sub-crate relies on an older version of a dependency for compatibility reasons, blindly updating might break its build or tests.
What about security? Should I run cargo audit every time after an update?

Absolutely. Running cargo audit is a good habit, especially since vulnerabilities can creep in through transitive dependencies. You might also consider integrating cargo deny into your CI pipeline to enforce stricter policies on allowed crates and patch versions.
I'm worried about false positives though. Sometimes it flags something that's actually safe just because of a known issue in the registry. How do I handle those?

You can use cargo deny with custom policies to whitelist specific crates or versions, effectively ignoring certain false positives while still catching real risks. Think of it like a security guard who lets trusted friends pass but stops strangers at the door.

I'm coming from Java and I keep thinking that putting a workspace root in Cargo.toml is just like declaring a project, but what exactly is the difference between a regular crate configuration and using it as a workspace root? When I define [workspace] do I actually lose the ability to configure individual members?

The key distinction is that a workspace root acts as an aggregation layer for multiple crates rather than defining a single binary or library crate. When you use [workspace], you are telling Cargo that this directory contains a collection of packages, and the members inherit default settings like edition and resolver versions unless they override them in their own [package] tables. This means you don't lose configuration power; instead, you gain centralized management where you can still define specific overrides per crate if needed. Since you're exploring systems programming, have you considered using cargo deny or cargo audit to check your workspace for security vulnerabilities across all members at once?

I'm back at coding after five years, and I'm stuck on this workspace inheritance gotcha. My Cargo.toml defines a workspace with multiple members, but when I run cargo update, it seems to ignore some of the dependency versions defined in the individual crate configs. Here is my setup: [workspace] members = ["app", "lib"]
[package]
name = "app"
description = "My app"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"

[[dependencies]]
name = "serde_json"
version = "=1.0.95"

[workspace.dependencies]
serde_json = "1.0.95"

I thought the workspace-level override should apply to all members, but cargo update only updates the direct dependencies in the root crate.

That's a classic misunderstanding of how Cargo resolves transitive dependencies versus explicit overrides. The issue is that when you define [[dependencies]] inside an individual [package] section, Cargo treats it as a local constraint for that specific crate only. When you use [workspace.dependencies], those are metadata used by tools like cargo update or cargo audit to find versions across the tree, but they don't automatically override direct dependencies unless explicitly referenced. To fix this, you need to ensure your app's Cargo.toml includes serde_json from the workspace table directly in its own [[dependencies]] section using the workspace name, like: [workspace] members = ["app", "lib"]
[package]
name = "app"
description = "My app"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2024"

[dependencies]
serde_json.workspace = true
